# Evaluating the Agentic Architect {#sec-evaluating-agentic-architect}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Measurement is the first step that leads to control and eventually to improvement. If you can’t measure something, you can’t understand it. If you can’t understand it, you can’t control it. If you can’t control it, you can’t improve it."*
>
> — H. James Harrington, *Business Process Improvement* (1991) [@Harrington1991BusinessProcess]
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** H. James Harrington, an author and quality engineer, emphasized that systemic improvement requires rigorous, well-defined measurement. In AI-assisted hardware design, the immediate temptation is to measure the final instructions-per-cycle (IPC) of the generated chip. But this conflates a lucky guess with systemic engineering capability. To build reliable systems, we must measure the *agent*, not just the *artifact*.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Preceding chapters verified the designs an AI produces and ran one bounded study to test a single AI-assisted claim. A parallel requirement now appears. We must evaluate the agent that drives the loop, not only the chip it emits. Software engineering already measures agents with execution-based benchmarks such as SWE-bench and ToolBench; computer architecture, with its vast state spaces and expensive simulators, needs its own. This chapter builds that evaluation in three movements. It first defines what to measure about the search itself (pass-rate, proxy calibration, trajectory efficiency, autonomous recovery, tool use, and context retention), then asks what makes a score trustworthy (contamination control, variance, and baselines), and finally turns a score into a decision (reliability, cost, and how much autonomy to grant). ArchEval offers a concrete instance of such a suite for computer architecture.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
Why is measuring the final performance of an AI-generated chip insufficient, and how do we rigorously evaluate the capability, efficiency, and reliability of the agent driving the design loop?
:::

The chapter answers this in three movements: what to measure about the search, whether the resulting score can be trusted, and how to turn that score into a decision. The three build on each other, because a metric is only as useful as the benchmark that produces it, and a benchmark is only useful if it changes what an architect does next.

## Why Measurement Changes When the Agent Enters the Loop

Before we can benchmark an AI architect, we should recall why human architects measure anything at all. Computer architecture is a strictly empirical discipline. We do not evaluate a processor by reading its source code; we evaluate it by running standardized workloads such as SPEC CPU or MLPerf and measuring the physical consequences, including IPC, power, area, and latency. Because building a physical chip costs millions of dollars and takes years, we measure these properties early and often using simulators and analytical models such as the Roofline model or McPAT. The goal of traditional architectural measurement is risk reduction. We measure to gain confidence that a design point will meet its physical requirements before we commit it to silicon.

When an AI agent enters the design loop, the object of measurement shifts. We are no longer only evaluating the *artifact*, the generated chip. We must now also evaluate the *agent*, the generative system driving the loop, because an agent can reach a correct architecture through incorrect and unscalable means.

The reason this matters is not aesthetic. A cheap search that stumbles onto a good design is perfectly acceptable; brute force is not a sin when it is affordable. The problem is that an expensive, unreasoned search does not *transfer*. An agent that found a five percent gain by luck cannot tell you which change caused it, cannot reproduce the result on the next workload, and cannot be trusted to know when to stop. In hardware this is expensive in a way software is not. In software, a stochastic search that eventually types a working script wastes ten milliseconds per try. In architecture, a search that burns ten thousand hours of cycle-accurate simulation to find a five percent gain destroys tape-out schedules and compute budgets. So we measure the *process* of design as a proxy for two things a single final score hides: whether a result will hold up out of distribution, and whether the agent can safely be given more autonomy. @tbl-eval-shift summarizes the shift.

| **Dimension** | **Evaluating the Artifact (Traditional)** | **Evaluating the Agent (Architecture 2.0)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **What is measured?** | The physical consequences of the processor. | The algorithmic efficiency of the search. |
| **Standard Benchmarks** | SPEC CPU, MLPerf, PARSEC. | ArchEval, SWE-bench, ToolBench. |
| **Primary Cost** | Paid once per design phase (NRE). | Paid iteratively during every loop (Simulator Tax). |
| **Failure Mode** | Thermal throttling, timing violations. | Hallucination loops, API misuse, context amnesia. |
| **Goal of Measurement** | Prove the chip works. | Prove the agent can reason causally. |

: **The paradigm shift in measurement.** Evaluating an AI architect requires a different set of metrics than evaluating the chip it produces. {#tbl-eval-shift tbl-colwidths="[20,40,40]"}

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can construct a trustworthy evaluation of an agentic architect. Specifically, you can:

- **Measure** the search itself: pass-rate, proxy calibration, trajectory efficiency, autonomous recovery, tool use, and long-horizon context retention;
- **Trust** a score by controlling for contamination, reporting variance across seeds, and comparing against a baseline at an equal simulator-call budget;
- **Decide** with the result by separating capability from reliability, placing agents on a cost-versus-quality frontier, and calibrating how much autonomy to grant;
- **Ground** each metric in the machine-learning idea that makes it necessary, from Goodhart's Law to the credit-assignment problem.
:::

## Lessons and Limits of Software Benchmarks

To establish how to benchmark agents, we can look to software engineering, where the machine-learning community has poured enormous effort into evaluating code generation. Benchmarks such as HumanEval [@ChenEtAl2021HumanEval] measure whether a model can generate a single functional Python snippet. More advanced frameworks such as SWE-bench [@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench] evaluate whether an agent can clone a real repository, parse a GitHub issue, and autonomously submit a patch that passes the project's hidden test suite, and tool-use benchmarks such as ToolBench [@QinEtAl2023ToolLLM] measure whether an agent can select and call external APIs correctly.

The first lesson from this work is the importance of *execution-based evaluation*. It is not enough to ask a model multiple-choice questions about Verilog syntax; the agent must run the code in a sandbox and be judged on what happens.

The second lesson is a warning, because software benchmarks are almost indifferent to *execution cost*. In SWE-bench, an agent that runs `pytest` five hundred times through brute-force print debugging is not penalized, as long as it eventually submits a working patch. Running five hundred Python unit tests takes thirty seconds. Running five hundred cycle-level gem5 simulations takes thirty days. So we keep the sandbox but must break from software by making *search efficiency* a first-class measure of success, not just functional correctness.

The third lesson is easy to miss because software benchmarks mostly handle it quietly. The test suite is *held out*. The agent never sees the grading tests, and the benchmark's credibility depends on that separation staying intact. Structural biology shows the payoff when it holds. CASP keeps its evaluation targets hidden until after predictions are submitted, so AlphaFold's reported accuracy measured genuine prediction rather than recall of known structures [@MoultEtAl1995CASP; @JumperEtAl2021AlphaFold]. That is the standard an architecture agent benchmark should aim for. Computer architecture has a harder version of this problem, which the third movement of this chapter takes up in detail.

## What to Measure: The Search, Not the Chip

If we cannot simply port SWE-bench into computer architecture, what exactly should we measure? Evaluating the process of design means tracking metrics across three of the eight roles introduced in @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization: **generation** (creating the design), **prediction** (estimating its value), and **optimization** (searching the space).

First, fix the unit of evaluation, because the metrics below assume it. A *task* is one bounded design problem posed to the agent. A *trajectory* is the sequence of proposals, tool calls, and observations the agent produces while answering that task. A *suite* is a set of tasks chosen to span difficulty and design domain. ArchEval [@WangEtAl2026ArchEval], a benchmark that scores LLM agents on twenty architecture challenges across three levels of simulator-feedback support, is one concrete instance of such a suite; the metrics in @tbl-agent-metrics are the quantities a suite like it records, and the failure mode each is designed to expose.

| **Metric** | **AI Role** | **What it Measures** | **Why we Measure it** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Pass-Rate ($Pass@k$)** | Generation | The fraction of proposals that clear validation without errors. | Detects the crudest reward hacking, catching illegal topologies before they can score. |
| **Proxy Calibration** | Prediction | The rank fidelity between the fast proxy and the slow simulator over the region the search visits. | Exposes the Proxy Mirage, where a globally accurate proxy still misranks the frontier. |
| **Trajectory Efficiency (AUC)** | Optimization | Reward quality as a function of environment interactions spent. | Exposes the Simulator Tax; shows whether the search is methodical or stochastic. |
| **Self-Correction (SCR)** | Generation & Optimization | The probability the agent resolves a failing error log on its next attempt. | Measures how well the agent resolves epistemic uncertainty. |
| **Context Retention** | Generation | How many turns the agent sustains before dropping an earlier constraint. | Exposes long-horizon context loss; tests whether the agent still honors a constraint set many turns earlier. |

: **Core metrics for evaluating the agentic architect.** A rigorous evaluation maps its metrics to the distinct roles of generation, prediction, and optimization. {#tbl-agent-metrics tbl-colwidths="[20,15,30,35]"}

The following sections take each metric in turn and ground it in the machine-learning idea that makes it necessary.

### Bounds Adherence and Reward Hacking

The most basic evaluation of an agent is whether it produces a valid proposal at all. In an open-topology search, generative models frequently propose illegal configurations. An agent might route a memory bus through a blocked macro, request an L2 cache that violates the SRAM area budget, or emit Verilog that does not compile.

**What to measure.** The zero-shot pass rate, meaning success on the first try, against the $k$-shot pass rate. The software estimator $Pass@k$ gives the probability that at least one of $k$ sampled candidates passes; it is computed without bias by drawing $n \ge k$ samples per task, counting the $c$ that pass, and averaging $1 - \binom{n-c}{k} / \binom{n}{k}$ over tasks. In architecture, a "pass" means the output clears the first layer of validation, for example a schema check on the JSON proposal or a `verilator --lint-only` compile.

**Why we measure it (Goodhart's Law and reward hacking).** Goodhart's Law holds that when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure [@Goodhart1975MonetaryManagement]. If we reward an agent only for maximizing IPC, it learns to reward hack: it proposes physically impossible chips, such as a cache larger than the die, because the analytical model mathematically predicts a large IPC gain. Measuring bounds adherence proves the agent is engineering within physical reality rather than exploiting the abstractions of the simulator.

### Proxy Calibration

Before an agent queries the slow simulator, it usually queries a fast surrogate model, a proxy, to predict the outcome. Evaluating the prediction role means measuring how much that proxy can be trusted.

**What to measure.** Rank fidelity where the search actually concentrates, not a global correlation. A proxy can track the simulator well on average, posting a high Pearson or Spearman coefficient across the whole space, and still reorder the handful of top candidates the optimizer will choose among. So measure top-$k$ rank agreement between the proxy and the simulator on the frontier the agent explores, or the regret from acting on the proxy's best point instead of the simulator's. Correlation is not calibration. What this metric scores is rank fidelity among the top candidates, not the probabilistic calibration of predicted confidences against observed frequencies that the term usually denotes in machine learning. A well-correlated proxy can be systematically wrong in exactly the region where the decision is made.

**Why we measure it (the Proxy Mirage).** A poorly calibrated proxy sends the optimizer confidently toward a Proxy Mirage, a region where the proxy predicts large gains but the simulator returns poor results. A global correlation hides exactly this failure, because it averages over the vast interior of the space where proxy and simulator agree and the optimizer never goes. Scoring rank fidelity where the search concentrates is what catches the mirage before the agent commits to it.

### Trajectory Efficiency and the Simulator Tax

An agent's practical value is set by its trajectory efficiency, how quickly it converges on a strong design instead of wandering.

**What to measure.** Not the final score alone, but the area under the curve (AUC) of the trajectory. Plot the number of environment round-trips (simulator calls) on the x-axis against the best reward seen so far on the y-axis. An efficient agent shows a steep early slope, reaching the target in few steps. Report alongside it the simulation-to-inference ratio, the time spent waiting on the environment against the time spent generating.

**Why we measure it (the Simulator Tax).** AI-alignment research speaks of an "alignment tax," the compute overhead of making a model safe. Architecture has a **Simulator Tax**: every unreasoned guess spends scarce simulation. If Agent A finds a five percent improvement in ten simulator calls and Agent B finds six percent but needs a thousand, Agent A is far more useful in practice. An evaluation must charge the Simulator Tax, favoring reasoned, methodical exploration over brute-force generation.

One caution runs beneath every metric here. We have written "reward" as a single number, but an architecture result is a vector of power, performance, area, and cost. Collapsing that vector into one score is a modeling choice, and it is itself a target the agent can hack, by trading away an unmeasured axis to inflate the measured one. An honest evaluation either scores on the Pareto front of the objectives or states the weighting it chose and checks the axes it suppressed. We return to this Pareto view in the third movement.

### Autonomous Recovery

When an architecture environment returns an error, such as a setup-time violation from OpenSTA or a routing-congestion map, the agent must parse that output and adjust its next proposal.

**What to measure.** The Self-Correction Rate (SCR):
$$ SCR = \frac{\text{Resolved Errors}}{\text{Total Environment Failures}} $$
Track alongside it the looping rate, the number of times an agent re-proposes the *same* failing design because it did not digest the feedback. Read the rate against the absolute error count and normalize by error difficulty as well, so an agent cannot inflate the ratio by manufacturing many trivially recoverable failures or by avoiding hard tasks entirely.

**Why we measure it (epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty).** In statistics, epistemic uncertainty can be reduced with more information and reasoning, while aleatoric uncertainty is irreducible noise. A synthesis error log carries deep causal information, which is epistemic. A strong agent reads the log, reduces its uncertainty, and fixes the missing semicolon or the congested route. A weak agent treats the log as aleatoric noise, ignores the text, and guesses again. The Self-Correction Rate measures the agent's capacity for causal reasoning directly.

### Tool Use and Rationale Faithfulness

Modern agents act by calling tools: invoking a synthesis script, querying a timing path, plotting a power map.

**What to measure.** The hallucinated-tool-call rate, meaning attempts to call tools absent from the environment schema, and the parameter-error rate, meaning passing a string where a float is required or requesting a path that does not exist.

**Why we measure it (the credit-assignment problem).** In reinforcement learning, the credit-assignment problem asks which of a thousand steps deserves credit for a reward that arrives only at the end. If an agent redesigns a whole SoC and achieves a ten percent speedup, was it the cache change, the ALU change, or the interconnect route? When an agent gathers evidence precisely, for example by querying the critical path with static timing analysis *before* touching the ALU, it shows that it knows why a design works and can assign credit as it goes.

Precise tool use, however, only proves the agent gathered the right evidence. It does not prove the agent's stated reason is the real one. An agent can query the critical path, modify the ALU, report a speedup, and then explain the win with a story about the cache that the trace does not support. This is the faithfulness question from @sec-feedback-verification-trust turned on the agent's own narration: does the recorded rationale match the recorded actions and observations? A fluent, confident, and wrong explanation is more dangerous than none, so an evaluation should check the rationale against the trajectory rather than accept it at face value.

### Context Retention

Hardware design is not a single-turn chat; it is a multi-week, stateful project. An agent might learn a constraint on the memory subsystem's latency on day one that it must apply when fixing a pipeline deadlock on day fourteen.

**What to measure.** Long-horizon tasks that inject a constraint early, for example "do not exceed a 10 mW power limit on the ALU," and then check whether the agent violates it many turns later. This is the architectural form of the needle-in-a-haystack evaluation.

**Why we measure it.** As context windows grow to millions of tokens, models still suffer amnesia, silently dropping earlier constraints as the log expands. AA-Briefcase [@ArtificialAnalysis2026Briefcase], a benchmark of multi-week knowledge-work deliverables, tests exactly this, grading work that depends on facts established much earlier in a large, messy input. Architecture benchmarks must do the same for deep RTL and constraint state.

## Whether to Trust the Measurement

A metric is only as trustworthy as the benchmark that produces it. Four failures make a good-looking score a lie: the tasks were contaminated, the number was noise, there was nothing to compare it against, or the metric was never shown to predict the capability it stands in for.

### Held-Out Tasks and Contamination

Software solved this quietly with hidden tests. Architecture cannot, because the failure runs deeper. The public corpus of RTL, configurations, and design reports is small, so the chance that a benchmark task already sat in a model's training data is high, and on a single score a memorized answer is indistinguishable from a reasoned one. A high pass-rate can measure recall, not capability.

**What to measure.** Whether the score survives contact with tasks the model could not have seen. The defenses are a hidden, human-validated task set; freshly authored or systematically perturbed tasks that could not have leaked; canary strings that reveal training-set inclusion after the fact; and a blunt diagnostic that any team can run, namely whether the score collapses when a task is reworded or swapped for a new one of equal difficulty. If it does, the benchmark was measuring memory.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Repairing a benchmark is not decontaminating it"}
**The claim.** SWE-bench quickly became the standard test of autonomous software engineering, and rising pass-rates on it were read as agents getting meaningfully better at real repository work [@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench].

**The gap.** A close audit found that a substantial fraction of the tasks were flawed as graded. Some issues were underspecified, some hidden tests were broken or far stricter than the issue implied, and the solution was sometimes recoverable from the issue text or the repository's own history rather than reasoned out. The public repositories also overlapped heavily with model pretraining data. The headline number therefore mixed genuine engineering with grading artifacts and memorization.

**The lesson.** The first response was to rebuild the grading rather than trust the score. OpenAI and the SWE-bench authors released SWE-bench Verified, a human-validated subset that removed the broken and underspecified tasks [@OpenAI2024SWEBenchVerified]. That fixed the tests but not the contamination. The tasks stayed public, frontier models had trained on the same repositories, and before long a high score again measured exposure rather than reasoning. Models reproduced the reference fixes from memory, roughly three in five of the still-failing tasks turned out to have broken tests, and in early 2026 OpenAI stopped evaluating against SWE-bench Verified and recommended a harder successor, SWE-bench Pro [@OpenAI2026SWEBenchLimits]. Within months it audited that successor too, found roughly a third of its tasks broken, and withdrew the recommendation [@OpenAI2026SWEBenchProAudit]. Human validation removes broken tasks, but it is not hold-out, and no one-time cleanup outruns contamination once a benchmark is public and models train on it. The durable defense is a continuously refreshed supply of held-out tasks, and even that stays provisional as each new set is exposed. Computer architecture, with a far smaller public corpus, starts this race behind, so genuinely novel tasks are not a refinement to add later; they are the price of a credible first number.
:::

### Variance, Seeds, and Replay

Agents are stochastic, and the hosted models behind them change without notice, so a single run is an anecdote, not a measurement. Two agents can swap places from one run to the next, and the same model identifier can behave differently a month later.

**What to measure.** Report the number of seeds, the sample size per task, and the spread of results, and put a confidence interval on any claimed gap rather than a bare difference of means. A bootstrap over the task set is the standard way to get that interval without assuming the scores are normally distributed [@Miller2024ErrorBars]. When several agents are compared at once, the same spread that makes one gap look real will make some comparison look significant by chance, so the bar for calling a difference meaningful must rise with the number of agents on the leaderboard. Reinforcement-learning evaluation learned this the hard way, where reported gains routinely vanished once results were re-run across new seeds and implementations [@HendersonEtAl2018DeepRL]. The remedy is the preregistration and replay discipline of @sec-running-the-loop applied to the agent itself: pin the model version and a response digest, fix the seeds and the budget before the run, and archive each trajectory so another team can replay it instead of trusting the reported number. Fix the agent scaffold too, meaning the prompt, retrieval, tool wrappers, and control loop wrapped around the base model, or report it as part of the result. In agent evaluation the scaffold often moves the score more than the base model does, so an unstandardized scaffold measures the harness rather than the agent's architectural reasoning.

### Baselines and Equal-Budget Comparison

A score means little without a floor and a ceiling. The floor is a human-expert baseline, what a competent architect achieves on the same task. The ceiling, where the space is small enough to enumerate, is the best design an exhaustive search would find. Between them, a pass-rate or an AUC becomes interpretable.

Just as important, agents of different cost must be compared at an equal simulator-call budget. This is exactly the equal-call design of the bounded study in @sec-executed-scale-study, where each arm received the same four-call budget and the question was which arm found more within it. Without a fixed budget, "Agent A beats Agent B" usually just means Agent A was allowed to spend more.

### Whether the Metrics Predict Capability

The first three failures assume each metric measures what its name claims. The deepest failure is that it may not. Pass-rate, trajectory AUC, and self-correction are stand-ins for a quantity no benchmark observes directly, whether the agent will produce a good design on a problem it has not seen. None of them is self-justifying. Goodhart's Law returned earlier for a single reward, and it returns here for the suite as a whole. Six process metrics are still a scoreboard, and an agent tuned to post efficient trajectories and clean self-corrections can climb it without getting better at architecture. The missing check is whether a high suite score actually predicts a good outcome. Hold out a set of design problems, run the suite, and test that link directly before trusting it. Until a process metric is shown to track downstream design quality, it reports how the agent searched, not how well it will do the job. A separate question is whether the suite itself is representative. Even a fully decontaminated suite whose metrics predict quality on its own tasks can overfit agent development to a narrow slice of architecture when its small, fixed task set does not cover the design space an agent will face in deployment.

A sharper failure appears when a system behaves well only while it senses that it is being measured. Volkswagen's diesel engines did exactly that, detecting the laboratory emissions test cycle and switching to a compliant mode they abandoned on the road [@Contag2017DefeatDevices]. An agent that could infer eval conditions from workload names, tool versions, or a fixed budget could game a suite the same way, passing the benchmark while behaving differently on real work. This is one more reason held-out and freshly authored tasks matter as much as the metrics computed on them.

## From Score to Decision

An evaluation exists to change what an architect does next. A leaderboard rank answers a research question; the practitioner deploying an agent has a sharper one, which is how much of the loop to let run unsupervised on a given task. Answering it requires reading the scores through reliability and cost, not peak capability.

### Capability Is Not Reliability

Pass@k rewards at least one success in $k$ tries, which is capability with retries. Deployment usually needs the opposite property, that the agent does not fail catastrophically when it matters.

**What to measure.** A reliability figure reported next to the optimistic ones: the worst-case outcome across runs, or the fraction of runs that finish with no constraint violation, a reliability@k rather than a best-of-$k$. An agent with a high peak and a fat tail of tape-out-bricking proposals is worse for real work than a duller, steadier one. Capability tells you what the agent can do on a good day; reliability tells you what it will do on a bad one, which is the number that governs how much you can hand it.

### The Cost-Quality Pareto

Because agents differ in both quality and cost, the honest comparison is a plane, not a ranking. @fig-agent-pareto places candidate agents by total evaluation compute, inference plus simulation, against the best design quality they reach, with the human baseline drawn as a reference line. The right question is not who wins outright but who sits on the frontier at the budget an organization can actually afford. A cheaper agent slightly below the best is often the correct deployment choice.

![**Comparing agents on a cost-quality plane.** Each agent is a point: total evaluation compute (inference plus simulation) against the best design quality it reaches. The non-dominated agents form a frontier; the human-expert baseline and a fixed compute budget turn "which agent is best" into "which agent is best at the budget we can afford."](images/F9-agent-pareto.svg){#fig-agent-pareto width="80%" fig-alt="A scatter plot of agents on axes of total evaluation compute versus design quality, with a Pareto frontier of non-dominated agents, a horizontal human-baseline line, and a vertical fixed-budget line."}

@fig-jevons-paradox shows why the cost axis cannot be waved away. Total evaluation compute is the sum of a *falling* simulation cost and a *rising* inference cost. A weak agent leans entirely on the simulator, querying it thousands of times to brute-force a working configuration; a frontier agent reasons more and simulates less but pays a large inference cost, since transformer inference scales steeply with model size. The sum is a U-shaped curve with a minimum, so the cheapest total evaluation sits at neither extreme.

![**The economics of agentic evaluation.** The total computational cost of evaluating an agent forms a U-shaped curve. Weak agents incur a large Simulator Tax through brute-force interaction. Frontier agents minimize simulation but incur a high inference cost. Evaluation must capture the total sum of this trade-off.](images/F9-compute-economics.pdf){#fig-jevons-paradox fig-alt="A U-shaped curve showing that total evaluation compute is a trade-off between inference cost and simulation cost." width=80%}

There is even a rebound effect, in the spirit of the Jevons Paradox, where greater efficiency raises total consumption because demand rises to meet it. As simulator interfaces become faster and cleaner, an agent simply calls them more often, so total cost need not fall as tools improve. A benchmark that charges only the inference cost or only the simulation cost misreads the economics of the loop; it must charge both.

### Granting Autonomy

The reliability figure and the cost frontier together answer the deployment question the leaderboard cannot. How much of this loop can run unsupervised, on this task, is a risk decision keyed to the reliability of the agent, the cost of a wasted run, and the reversibility of the next action. A steady agent proposing a cache-size sweep behind a cheap proxy check can be given a long leash; a peaky agent one approval away from authorizing a tape-out cannot. This is where @tbl-eval-checklist earns its place, and it points forward to @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack and @sec-what-architect-owns, where the operating conditions of the loop and the ownership boundary decide what an evaluation is ultimately allowed to automate.

| **Check** | **What a trustworthy agent evaluation reports** |
| --- | --- |
| **Search quality** | Pass@k, proxy calibration, trajectory AUC, self-correction rate, tool precision, and context retention, each mapped to a role. |
| **Cost** | Total evaluation compute as inference plus simulation, not one in isolation. |
| **Contamination** | A held-out, human-validated task set, and a score that survives reworded or freshly authored tasks. |
| **Variance** | Seeds, sample size, spread, a confidence interval on any claimed gap, and whether it survives comparison across agents, with the model version pinned and the agent scaffold fixed or disclosed. |
| **Baseline and budget** | A human-expert floor and comparison at an equal simulator-call budget. |
| **Validity** | Evidence that a high suite score predicts good outcomes on held-out design problems, not just efficient search. |
| **Reliability** | A worst-case or reliability@k figure beside the best-of-$k$ number. |
| **Faithfulness** | The agent's stated rationale checked against its recorded trajectory. |

: **A checklist for a trustworthy agent evaluation.** A benchmark that reports only the top row measures capability on a good day; the remaining rows are what make the number safe to act on. {#tbl-eval-checklist tbl-colwidths="[25,75]"}

## Open Research Questions

The metrics above are a starting point, and each leaves an open problem where AI's role is explicit.

- **Contamination-resistant hardware benchmarks.** Software can refresh its tasks from a large, growing corpus of public code. Architecture cannot, because the public corpus of RTL and design reports is small. What generation or perturbation method can produce architecture tasks that are provably novel to a given model, so that a score measures reasoning rather than recall?
- **A standard total-cost metric.** Trajectory efficiency and the Simulator Tax both depend on a shared unit that combines inference and simulation cost. What normalization lets an agent that reasons more but simulates less be compared fairly against one that does the reverse, across different hardware and model sizes?
- **Measuring rationale faithfulness at scale.** Checking a single explanation against a single trajectory is tractable by hand. What automated check can decide, across thousands of trajectories, whether an agent's stated causal story matches the evidence it actually gathered? Any such check is likely a model in its own right, so what keeps the grader from sharing the blind spots of the agent it audits?
- **Attributing credit across a team of agents.** The metrics here evaluate a single agent, yet real loops compose a generator, a critic, and a verifier. When such a team succeeds or fails, which agent's contribution can an evaluation isolate, and what signal must the environment record to make that attribution possible?
- **Certifying reliability for autonomy.** Granting autonomy is a risk decision, but there is no accepted standard for how much reliability evidence justifies how much unsupervised authority. What worst-case guarantee, over what task distribution, should an organization require before an agent may run a step without human sign-off?

## Conclusion and Carry Forward {#sec-conclusion-and-carry-forward}

Evaluating an agentic architect is a different discipline from evaluating a chip, and it takes three movements to do well. We measure the search rather than only the artifact, using pass-rate, proxy calibration, trajectory efficiency, self-correction, tool use, and context retention. The chip remains the ground truth, and we measure the search precisely because that ground truth is too expensive to sample often, so the search metrics stay anchored to design quality through the validity check rather than replacing it. We then make the score trustworthy by controlling for contamination, reporting variance across seeds, and comparing against a baseline at an equal budget. Finally we turn the score into a decision, reading reliability and cost to judge how much of the loop an agent may run on its own. Grounding each metric in its underlying idea, from Goodhart's Law and the Simulator Tax to epistemic uncertainty and credit assignment, keeps the evaluation honest about what a high number does and does not prove. ArchEval shows that a suite of this kind is now buildable for computer architecture; the harder work is keeping it trustworthy as the agents it measures learn to game it.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Evaluating the agent is distinct from evaluating the hardware. Measure the search (pass-rate, trajectory efficiency, self-correction, tool use, context retention), make the score trustworthy (contamination control, variance, an equal-budget baseline), and decide with it (reliability and cost, not peak capability).
- **Reader test:** Can you explain why an agent that treats simulator error logs as aleatoric noise instead of epistemic uncertainty will incur a large Simulator Tax, and why a high pass-rate on a contaminated benchmark tells you nothing about that agent's reasoning?
- **Up next:** @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack examines how these evaluated agents are deployed, and how the study structure changes across layers of the stack, from microarchitecture to physical design.
:::
